guideline_references: EAU Guidelines on Chronic Pelvic Pain 2024
Therapeutic Guidelines: Urological (chronic pelvic pain / chronic prostatitis) 2024
RACGP: management of male chronic pelvic pain / CP-CPPS
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: an alarming presentation (severe escalating perineal pain, painful and difficult voiding, an 'urgent after-hours' framing) whose true cause is a benign chronic pelvic-pain flare — the source note's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE and its disposition routine (GP review in 1 week). The scored defect is escalating to 000/ambulance/ED on the alarming surface features when no positive red flag exists.

Narrow telehealth-limit uplift: the note's negative screen was achieved by IN-PERSON digital rectal examination, testicular examination, bladder percussion and urine dipstick — none of which Breath-Ezy can perform. Acute urinary retention, acute bacterial prostatitis and testicular torsion are plausible serious mimics that genuinely cannot be excluded over chat and would need urgent in-person management if present, so the correct telehealth disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), not home self-care.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours home visit; all examination/urinalysis findings are reprojected to sealed clinician-only nodes and the AI Doctor is scored on recognise-likely-benign-cause + arrange in-person exam/urinalysis + safety-net, never on performing the exam.

Premature-closure / anchoring trap: accepting a 'chronic pelvic pain flare, just use heat packs' label and managing purely at home without an examination or urine test — which would miss retention or bacterial prostatitis — is critical under-triage.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source note CFE-008.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
